summary: Cathy O'Neil shares her personal experience with anxiety-induced insomnia, noting that while she used to suffer from depression, having children shifted her struggles toward anxiety. She describes her inability to fantasize or let go of concrete problems, leading to sleepless nights once or twice a week. Rather than offering a cure, she provides practical coping mechanisms including accepting worry without meta-worry, listening to boring radio or baseball games, using a pillow speaker to avoid disturbing her husband, and watching intentionally dull Netflix content. She enthusiastically recommends a PBS documentary about deer as the ultimate sleep aid.
key_points: The transition from depression to anxiety after having children changed the nature of her mental health struggles
Her analytical mind that focuses on concrete problems makes traditional relaxation techniques like fantasy visualization ineffective
Accepting sleeplessness without added worry about being worried is a key coping strategy
Boring content (sports radio, baseball games, nature documentaries) serves as an effective distraction from anxiety
Practical accommodations like pillow speakers can help manage anxiety without disrupting partners
money_quotes: But ever since I had kids, I suffer much more from anxiety.
I basically have no imagination and spend my brain cycles trying to solve really concrete problems, and if there's something out of my control then it bugs me and I have trouble letting go.
First of all, don't worry about being worried.
Chances are the next night you will be super exhausted and catch up on sleep, no harm done.
Sports radio is almost always soothingly boring.
Of course an actual baseball game is perfect, because nothing ever happens.
key_insight: For analytical minds that can't simply 'let go,' the best sleep strategy isn't fighting anxiety but redirecting attention toward sufficiently boring content.
